exercises.title: Why schema design drives tool-selection accuracy
exercises.prompt: A correct tool fails silently when the model can't tell when to use it. Explain why naming, descriptions, and parameter shapes drive 10-20 point swings in tool-selection accuracy, and the 'Use when X. Do not use for Y.' description pattern.
exercises.hint: The model picks tools from the schema text alone. Ambiguous names/descriptions → wrong tool or no tool (silent failure). 'Use when X. Do not use for Y.' tells the model both when to fire AND when not to — disambiguating from similar tools.
exercises.solution:
**Why schema design drives tool-selection accuracy:** the model decides **which tool to call from the schema text alone** — the name, description, and parameter definitions are *all* it sees (Phase 13.1's describe step). If those are ambiguous, the model **mis-fires**: it calls the **wrong tool**, calls **no tool** when it should, or calls a tool with **bad arguments**. Crucially, this is a **silent failure** — the tool itself is correct and runs fine; the problem is the model **chose wrong** because the schema didn't make the tool's purpose clear. Benchmarks like **StableToolBench and MCPToolBench++** measure **10-20 percentage-point swings** in tool-selection accuracy purely from naming/description/parameter quality — schema design is **as important as the tool's implementation**. A perfectly-coded tool with a vague description is worthless if the model never picks it (or picks it wrongly).

**The 'Use when X. Do not use for Y.' pattern:** a good tool description states **both when to use the tool AND when not to**:
```
get_weather: "Get the CURRENT weather for a city. 
             Use when the user asks about present conditions or temperature. 
             Do NOT use for forecasts (use get_forecast) or historical weather."
```
**Why both halves matter:**
- **'Use when X'** tells the model the **positive trigger** — the situations where this tool is the right choice. Without it, the model may not recognize when the tool applies.
- **'Do not use for Y'** tells the model the **negative boundary** — when *not* to fire, especially to **disambiguate from similar tools**. If you have `get_weather` and `get_forecast`, the model needs to know the **difference** — 'do not use for forecasts' steers it to the right one. Without negative guidance, the model confuses similar tools (the main cause of mis-fires in a registry of overlapping tools).
Keep it **under ~1024 characters** (descriptions consume context, Phase 11.5 / Lesson 2's limits), **concrete, and unambiguous**. The description is the model's **only instruction manual** for the tool — write it for the model, with explicit positive and negative triggers, and tool-selection accuracy jumps.

exercises.title: Naming, atomic vs monolithic tools, and a schema linter
exercises.prompt: Explain how to name tools (stable, snake_case, unambiguous across a large registry), the tradeoff between atomic tools and one monolithic tool, and implement a tool-schema linter that flags common problems. When does each design win?
exercises.hint: Names: snake_case, verb_noun, stable, distinct across the registry. Atomic: many small single-purpose tools (clear, but registry bloat + selection harder). Monolithic: one tool with a mode param (compact, but the model must pick the mode). Linter: check name format, description length, 'use when' presence, param descriptions, enums.

exercises.title: Parameter design, error feedback, and schema design as a system-wide concern
exercises.prompt: Explain how parameter design (constraints, descriptions, defaults, enums) improves both selection and argument-generation accuracy, how tool error messages should feed back to the model, and why tool-schema design is a registry-wide system concern (overlap, naming consistency, namespacing) that interacts with MCP, security, and evaluation.
exercises.hint: Params: enums/constraints/descriptions guide the model to valid args; defaults reduce required fields. Errors: return actionable messages the model can act on (typed repair). Registry-wide: consistent naming, no overlapping tools, namespacing across servers. Connects to MCP discovery, tool-poisoning (descriptions are attack surface), and tool-selection eval.
exercises.solution:
**Parameter design (improves both selection AND argument-generation):**
- **Constraints (enum, min/max, pattern):** an `enum` for a fixed-choice param (`currency: USD|EUR|GBP`) both **guides the model to valid values** and **constrains decoding** (Lesson 4) — it can't generate an invalid currency. Ranges and patterns similarly prevent bad arguments. Constraints turn 'hope the model produces valid args' into 'the model produces valid args.'
- **Parameter descriptions:** each param needs a **clear description with an example** ('`city`: the city name, e.g. "Paris"') — the model generates better arguments when it understands each parameter's meaning and format. Undescribed params are a common cause of malformed calls.
- **Defaults / optionality:** marking params optional (with sensible defaults handled in the executor) **reduces the number of required fields** the model must produce, lowering the chance of a malformed call. Fewer required fields = simpler, more reliable calls.
- **Net effect:** good parameter design improves **argument-generation accuracy** (valid, well-formed args) *and* indirectly **selection** (a tool with clear, constrained params signals its purpose better). It's the second half of schema design (after naming/description).

**Tool error messages should feed back to the model:** when a tool call fails (bad args, not found, downstream error), the **error returned as the observation should be actionable** — 'city "Pariss" not found, did you mean "Paris"?' or 'quantity must be ≥ 1' — so the model can **self-correct on the next turn** (typed repair, Phase 13.1/11.3). Cryptic errors ('error 500') give the model nothing to act on; **specific, instructive errors** let it fix the call. Treat tool errors as **instructions to the model**, not just logs — they're part of the tool's interface and drive recovery.

**Why schema design is a registry-wide system concern:** individual tools must be well-designed, but the **registry as a whole** must be coherent:
- **Overlap/disambiguation:** tools with **overlapping purposes** are the main cause of mis-selection — the registry needs **clear boundaries** ('use when / do not use for Y') so the model can tell similar tools apart. You design tools **relative to each other**, not in isolation.
- **Naming consistency:** a **consistent naming scheme** (`verb_noun`, snake_case) across the whole registry makes the model's selection more reliable and the registry maintainable.
- **Namespacing across servers:** when **multiple MCP servers** (Lesson 8) merge into one tool namespace, **name collisions** (two `search` tools) must be resolved (server prefixes/namespacing) or the client/model can't route correctly. Registry-wide naming discipline is essential at scale.
- **Count/context budget:** the **total number and size of tool schemas** consume context and degrade selection (Lesson 2's limits) — the registry must be **curated** (don't expose 200 tools; expose the relevant subset, possibly dynamically).

**How it interacts with MCP, security, and evaluation:**
- **MCP (Lesson 6+):** MCP **standardizes tool discovery and schemas** — your well-designed schemas become MCP tool definitions that **any client discovers**. Schema design quality directly determines how reliably *any* MCP host can use your tools, and MCP's `tools/list` is where naming/description discipline pays off across the ecosystem. Resources vs tools (Lesson 10) is itself a schema-design decision (expose a read as a resource, not a tool).
- **Security / tool poisoning (Lesson 15):** **tool descriptions land in the model's context verbatim** — which makes them an **attack surface**. A malicious server can embed **hidden instructions in a description** (tool poisoning). So schema design isn't just about clarity — **descriptions are security-sensitive** (they must be **hash-pinned and scanned**, Lesson 15), and you must treat third-party tool schemas as **untrusted**. Good schema design (and verification) is part of the security posture.
- **Evaluation:** **tool-selection accuracy is measurable** (StableToolBench, MCPToolBench++, your own eval) — you should **evaluate the registry** (does the model pick the right tool for representative queries?) and **regression-test** schema changes (a description tweak can swing selection 10-20 points, Lesson 1's 'prompts are code' applied to tool schemas). Schema design must be **measured and iterated**, not guessed.
**The synthesis:** tool-schema design is a **first-class engineering discipline** — naming, descriptions ('use when/do not use'), parameter constraints, error feedback, and **registry-wide coherence** (overlap, consistency, namespacing, count) together drive **10-20 points of tool-selection and argument-generation accuracy**, and they interact with the **whole tools stack**: MCP standardizes and distributes your schemas, security treats descriptions as an attack surface to verify, and evaluation measures whether the model actually picks correctly. A correct tool is necessary but not sufficient — the **schema is the interface the model reasons over**, so designing it well (and linting/evaluating it) is as important as implementing the tool itself.
